_meta.note: book quiz teaser · BuzzFeed tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: The Selfish Gene Dopamine Master Quiz · Richard Dawkins
html_002: The real hero of evolution isn’t the individual — it’s the gene
html_004: “We are survival machines — robot vehicles blindly
html_005: programmed to preserve the selfish molecules known as genes.”
html_007: Master evolution seen through the gene’s eyes — its key ideas, cases, and debates — in
html_009:
.
        You start easy and it ramps up,
html_010: and every miss comes back until you nail it.
html_027: That’s the end of the free preview
html_030: You saw right through the gene
html_040: The Selfish Gene at a glance
html_043: and you’ll never forget it. Every starting point is the
html_047: 🧫 The self-replicator in the primordial soup
html_048: The first molecule that copies itself
html_051: Competing to make more copies of itself
html_053: 🤖 Building & steering a survival machine (the body)
html_054: The body is the gene’s vehicle
html_056: 🤝 Strategies of altruism (kin selection · ESS · reciprocity)
html_057: The cooperation a “selfish” gene gives rise to
html_059: 🧠 A new replicator, the “meme” + humans who defy their genes
html_060: “We can rebel against our selfish genes”
html_062: Table of contents at a glance
html_064: 3 cases from the book worth pondering
html_066: Focused review of what you missed
html_068: lets you dig deeper into the key concepts and debates in The Selfish Gene.
html_074: A repeat-master quiz that turns a book’s core into dopamine
html_075: This app reworks the book’s key ideas into secondary learning content for study purposes. For the full reading experience, please pick up the original.

js_004: Which British evolutionary biologist wrote The Selfish Gene?
js_009: The Selfish Gene is a 1976 popular-science book by British evolutionary biologist Richard Dawkins. It made the world see evolution through ‘the eyes of the gene’.
js_011: In which year did this book first appear?
js_016: The Selfish Gene first came out from Oxford University Press in 1976. A revised edition followed in 1989, the 30th-anniversary edition in 2006, and the 40th in 2016.
js_018: What does the book see as the fundamental unit of natural selection?
js_023: Dawkins saw the real unit that natural selection acts on as the gene — not the individual or the species. Genes compete to survive.
js_025: What did Dawkins call our bodies (individual organisms)?
js_029: Dawkins called the body a survival machine, built and steered by genes. The body is basically a ‘vehicle’ that carries genes around.
js_031: What word — coined and spread by Dawkins in this book — means a unit of cultural transmission?
js_035: A meme is a ‘cultural replicator’ — songs, catchphrases, fashions that copy from person to person. Dawkins coined it in Chapter 11 of this book, echoing the word ‘gene’.
js_037: What is the title actually calling ‘selfish’?
js_040: The ‘selfish’ in the title points to the gene, not to humans. It’s a metaphor: genes act as if they’re trying to make more copies of themselves — it doesn’t mean genes have minds.
js_042: What key term for the gene means ‘a thing that copies itself’?
js_046: Dawkins calls the gene a self-replicator. The history of life, he says, is the story of these replicators competing to survive by copying themselves exactly.
js_048: Where does Dawkins say the first self-replicator arose by chance?
js_052: In the sea before life began — the so-called primordial soup — a molecule that copied itself appeared by chance. The competition among these replicators is where evolution begins.
js_054: The body dies, but the gene lives on through copies. Which phrase best matches how Dawkins describes it?
js_056: Lives just one generation, then vanishes
js_059: Dawkins calls the gene ‘the immortal coil’. The body vanishes within a generation, but the gene can survive for millions of years through its copies.
js_061: To the question of whether humans are ruled by their selfish genes forever, what’s Dawkins’ answer?
js_062: Humans alone can rebel against the gene’s rule
js_063: Humans are just puppets of their genes too
js_065: Even civilization runs on the genes’ orders
js_066: Dawkins’ conclusion is surprisingly hopeful. Only humans who understand the logic of the selfish gene can defy its commands and choose to live altruistically.
js_073: EBS Class-e: Reading The Selfish Gene (KR)
js_074: Richard Dawkins (b. 1941) is an evolutionary biologist and former Oxford professor. The Selfish Gene made him world-famous; he went on to write The Blind Watchmaker, The God Delusion, and more.
js_075: After the 1976 first edition, Dawkins added two new chapters in the 1989 revised edition — ‘Nice Guys Finish First’ and ‘The Long Reach of the Gene’. That’s the version widely read today.
js_076: The core question is ‘what gets selected?’ Dawkins argued the unit of natural selection is the gene, not the individual or species. The individual is just a vessel the gene briefly inhabits.
js_077: A survival machine is the body genes build to protect and carry themselves. Our eyes, hands, brains, and behavior are all devices honed to help genes survive.
js_078: ‘Meme’ comes from a Greek word for ‘imitation’, shaped to sound like ‘gene’. Like songs, catchphrases, and technologies, it’s a cultural replicator that copies from brain to brain.
js_079: ‘Selfish’ is a metaphor aimed at the gene. It’s not that genes have desires — they just ‘appear to act’ in ways that favor leaving more copies of themselves.
js_080: A replicator is anything that copies itself. Dawkins rewrites the history of life as ‘the story of replicators competing to survive’.
js_081: The primordial soup is the chemical broth of the pre-life sea. In it, a self-copying molecule arose by chance, and whichever copied better survived — and evolution began.
js_082: The body disappears within a generation, but the gene carries on through copies. That’s why Dawkins calls the gene ‘the immortal coil’ — a thing that practically never dies.
js_083: Dawkins’ conclusion is hopeful. Humans who grasp the logic of the selfish gene can ‘choose’ to live altruistically, against the selfishness their genes push for.
js_096: ↻ This one will come back later
js_098: The real unit of natural selection is not the individual or the species — it’s the gene. A gene is a self-replicator competing to leave copies of itself.
js_100: Our body is a survival machine that genes build and steer — a vehicle that carries genes around.
js_101: Relatives share genes with you. Helping relatives means helping your own genes — so altruism toward family evolves.
js_103: Like the hawk–dove game, it’s a stable pattern of behavior that can’t be overturned even when a rival strategy invades.
js_105: If you’ll meet again, helping each other and paying it back benefits both sides’ genes. It’s the root of cooperation between strangers.
js_106: A cultural replicator — songs, catchphrases, fashions — that copies from brain to brain. Dawkins coined the word in this book.
js_107: Like a beaver’s dam, a gene’s effects reach beyond the body into the environment. A gene’s power isn’t trapped inside the body.
js_108: Humans who understand the selfish gene are the only beings that can defy its commands and live altruistically.
js_110: Why we exist — the birth of the replicator
js_111: To the question ‘why do we live?’, Dawkins looks for the answer through the eyes of the gene.
js_112: In the pre-life primordial soup, a molecule that copies itself — the replicator — first appears.
js_113: Replicators that copy more accurately, faster, and longer survive and multiply.
js_114: Today’s descendants of that replicator are exactly what we call genes.
js_116: The immortal coil & the survival machine
js_117: The body vanishes within a generation, but the gene leaps across millions of years through copies.
js_118: The gene builds and steers a body to carry it — the ‘survival machine’.
js_119: Brains, muscles, and behavior are all devices honed to help the gene survive.
js_120: The view that the body is the gene’s ‘vehicle’ is the heart of this book.
js_123: The hawk–dove game explains why aggression stops at a certain line (ESS).
js_124: Relatives share your genes, so helping a relative is helping your own genes.
js_125: Hamilton’s rule, rB > C — when benefit × relatedness beats the cost, altruism evolves.
js_126: Even a parent’s love for its child ultimately comes down to a gene’s-eye ‘calculation’.
js_128: Family planning & the war of generations and sexes
js_129: Animals don’t just breed as much as possible — they adjust their number of offspring to what they can raise.
js_130: Offspring want a bit more while parents want to share evenly — so weaning conflict arises.
js_131: The split between the large, costly egg and the small, cheap sperm sets the sexes’ strategies apart.
js_132: The female’s caution and the male’s competition — the game of push and pull starts here.
js_134: Cooperation by paying it back, and the meme
js_135: If you’ll meet again, reciprocal altruism — helping and paying back — evolves.
js_136: In Chapter 11, Dawkins first proposes the cultural replicator, the ‘meme’.
js_137: Like songs, catchphrases, fashions, and tech, memes also go through copying, mutation, and competition.
js_138: Memes spread far faster than genes, from just about anyone to anyone.
js_140: Nice guys finish first · The long reach of the gene
js_141: In the repeated prisoner’s dilemma, tit-for-tat — which ‘cooperates first’ — wins.
js_142: A ‘nice’ strategy that never defects first turns out to pay off best in the long run.
js_143: Like a beaver’s dam, a gene’s effects reach out into the world beyond the body (the extended phenotype).
js_144: Yet humans alone can understand the gene’s selfishness — and stand against it.
js_145: The worker bee that gives its life
js_146: A worker bee that stings dies, but it defends the hive. As an individual it’s suicide — but since the queen and sisters share its genes, from the ‘gene’s eye’ it pays off.
js_147: That an individual’s sacrifice can benefit its genes — this case shows at a glance why kin selection is so powerful.
js_148: The cuckoo that dumps its eggs in another’s nest
js_149: A cuckoo secretly lays its egg in another bird’s nest, and its chick shoves out the original eggs. The host bird, tricked into raising a stranger’s chick, is being manipulated by cuckoo genes.
js_150: One creature’s genes can even change another creature’s behavior — it makes you think about the ‘extended phenotype’ and the arms race between genes.
js_151: The vampire bat that shares its blood
js_152: A vampire bat regurgitates the blood it drank to share with a starving companion. This sharing holds up on a footing of paying it back — give today, get it back when you’re the hungry one.
js_153: Even without kinship, cooperation evolves as long as ‘paying it back’ works — the classic example of reciprocal altruism.
js_154: If my body is the gene’s ‘survival machine’, then what on earth is the ‘me’?
js_155: Genes made you — but it’s also you who read this book and now ‘know’ that. Think about how far that knowing can change you.
js_156: How can ‘altruistic’ behavior come out of a ‘selfish’ gene?
js_157: Think of kin selection, reciprocity, and paying it back. It’s worth chewing on the paradox that selfishness and cooperation actually spring from the same root.
js_158: If memes are ‘selfish’ just like genes, are the thoughts in my head really mine?
js_159: Some beliefs and trends may have stuck around not because they benefit you, but simply because they ‘spread well’. Ask yourself who your convictions really serve.
js_160: 🧬 You saw right through the gene
js_161: You read The Selfish Gene flawlessly
js_162: 🔬 A reader who cracked the replicator’s logic
js_163: You’ve got almost all of the core
js_164: 🧫 You kept pace with the evolution game
js_165: You’ve learned the framework — one more round!
js_166: 🌱 Just crawled out of the primordial soup
js_167: The more you repeat, the higher your score
